structure.title: Preface
structure.summary:
This partial document, published by the Marin County Stormwater Pollution Prevention Program (MCSTOPPP), is a guide promoting the use of native plants for yards, patios, and creeks in Marin County. It covers the numerous benefits of using native plants, such as their adaptation to local climate and soil, ability to attract beneficial insects and wildlife, low water requirements, and resistance to pests and diseases, which helps reduce the need for toxic pesticides and protects stormwater quality.

The document provides practical advice on planting natives, including tips for establishment, ideal planting times, and where to purchase them. It features various native plant lists tailored for specific purposes, such as attracting wildlife, deer resistance, drought tolerance, and fire resistance. Additionally, it offers guidance on planting container gardens and specific instructions for planting along creeks, including a list of suitable creekside plants and warnings against invasive species. The document also addresses the issue of pest plants in Marin and the broader impact of pesticides on water quality.

structure.title: Why Use Native Plants?

nodes.title: Tips on Planting “Natives”
nodes.summary: The partial document describes the benefits of using native plants in gardening, emphasizing their adaptation to local climate and soil, their role in supporting local wildlife (insects, birds, butterflies), and their low maintenance requirements (less watering, pest resistance). It clarifies the importance of selecting plants truly native to a specific local area and provides tips for planting and establishing them, such as initial care and ideal planting times. A substantial part of the document is dedicated to listing various nurseries and other sources, both local and regional, where native plants can be purchased, along with contact information. It also points to an external website for more information.

nodes.title: Where to Buy Natives
nodes.summary: The partial document provides guidance on acquiring and planting native plants and wildflowers. It lists numerous nurseries and organizations where native plants can be purchased, categorized by local and other locations, including online options and plant sales. The document also offers advice on the best time to plant native species (fall) and their initial watering needs. Furthermore, it discusses selecting native plants that attract beneficial wildlife, emphasizing the importance of choosing species appropriate for specific local climates. Finally, it provides detailed instructions for sowing California native wildflower seeds, covering timing, site preparation, seed mixing, sowing methods, and post-sowing care.

nodes.title: When and What to Plant
Sowing Wildflower Seeds
nodes.summary: The partial document provides guidance on planting native plants, including the best time (fall) and initial care requirements. It offers specific instructions for sowing California wildflower seeds, covering site preparation, seed mixing, sowing techniques, and post-sowing care. Additionally, it features a detailed list of California native plants, noting their form, blooming season, and their effectiveness in attracting various wildlife such as butterflies, beneficial insects, hummingbirds, and backyard birds, with an indication of their native status to Marin.

This partial document provides guidance on planting native plants, focusing on optimal timing (fall before rains) and initial care requirements for the first year or two. It discusses selecting native plants beneficial for attracting wildlife like butterflies and birds, emphasizing the importance of choosing plants truly native to specific local climates within California and Marin County. The document also offers detailed instructions for sowing California native wildflower seeds, covering aspects like timing, site preparation, seed mixing, scattering techniques, and post-sowing care, including encouraging natural re-seeding.

nodes.title: Native Plant List–Plants that Attract Wildlife
nodes.summary: This partial document is a list of California native plants, presented in a table format. It provides information for each plant including its common name, botanical name, growth form (perennial, annual, tree, shrub, or vine), and blooming season. A key feature of the list is its focus on plants that attract wildlife, with indicators for whether a plant attracts backyard birds, hummingbirds, beneficial insects, or butterflies, and if it is native to Marin. The document aims to guide gardeners in selecting reliable and hardy native plants to enhance their gardens and support local wildlife.

nodes.title: Deer Resistant Native Plants
nodes.summary: This partial document discusses the variability of deer resistance in plants, noting that deer preferences can change based on location, time of year, and the deer's age. It provides a link to a resource for more information on deer-resistant plants, including "best bets." The document then emphasizes the benefits of using native plants in landscaping due to their adaptation to local climates and offers a list of specific native plants considered deer-resistant, with those "almost always deer resistant" highlighted in bold. It also clarifies that the designation "some" next to a plant name indicates that only certain subspecies are native to California.

nodes.title: Drought Tolerant Native Plants
nodes.summary: This partial document describes drought-tolerant native plants, explaining their characteristics and distinguishing them from water-loving or seasonally compatible native species. It provides extensive lists of various native drought-tolerant plants, categorized into seasonal compatible plants, shrubs, and grasses/groundcovers. Additionally, the document offers resources for water-wise gardening, including a free CD from the Marin Municipal Water District, a list of stores selling water-wise plants, and details about a rebate program from the North Marin Water District for replacing turf with drought-tolerant native plants. Contact information for both water districts is provided for further inquiries.

nodes.title: Fire Resistant Plants
nodes.summary: This partial document emphasizes the importance of choosing fire-resistant plants and creating defensible space to protect properties from wildfires, citing past California fires as a catalyst. It outlines key characteristics of fire-resistant plants, such as being broad-leaf deciduous, having supple leaves, and being clean with minimal deadwood. The document also provides a list of specific firewise native plants and defines defensible space. Finally, it directs readers to FireSafe Marin for more information and resources on wildfire prevention.

nodes.title: Beneficial Insects
nodes.summary: This partial document covers several key gardening topics: the importance of beneficial insects for natural pest control, including a list of common beneficial insects and their prey, along with native plants that attract them and resources for further information. It also provides lists of native perennial, annual, and grass plants suitable for sunny garden areas. Finally, it offers advice on planting and selecting plants for container gardens, specifically mentioning native plant choices.

nodes.title: Planting a Container Garden
nodes.summary: This partial document provides comprehensive guidance on selecting and planting various native plants based on specific environmental conditions and gardening purposes. It includes detailed lists of perennials, annuals, grasses, and shrubs suitable for sunny areas and part shade, often with both common and scientific names. The document also offers advice on planting container gardens, recommending suitable plant types. A significant section is dedicated to planting along creeks, explaining the ecological benefits of riparian corridors, emphasizing the use of native species, and listing specific native trees appropriate for different Marin County watersheds. It concludes with a reminder about the potential need for permits for creekside restoration projects.

nodes.title: Planting Along Creeks
Marin Native Creekside Plant List
nodes.summary: This partial document provides a comprehensive list of native plants suitable for creekside environments in Marin County, categorized by plant type (ground covers, perennials, ferns & vines, grasses & grass-like plants, shrubs, trees) and their ideal location on a creek bank (low, middle, or high). Each entry includes the plant's common name, scientific name, typical size, and sun/shade requirements. Additionally, the document emphasizes the importance of avoiding and removing invasive "pest plants" near creeks and open spaces, explaining their negative impacts on bank stability and native ecosystems, and offering guidance on their removal and replacement with native species to prevent erosion.

nodes.title: Avoid Invasive Plants Near Creeks
nodes.summary: This partial document provides guidance on managing vegetation near creeks and open spaces, focusing on the importance of removing invasive "pest plants" and replacing them with native species. It explains that invasive plants destabilize banks, outcompete natives, and offer poor wildlife habitat. The text advises removing invasives in small sections and immediately replanting with natives to prevent erosion. Specific examples of invasive plants to avoid are listed, including Acacia, Bamboo, Spanish broom, and Tree-of-Heaven. The document also promotes planting willows as an effective and inexpensive method for creek bank stabilization and habitat creation, and directs readers to the Marin County Stormwater Pollution Prevention Program (MCSTOPPP) for technical assistance and more information.

nodes.title: Planting Willows
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the benefits of planting willows for creek bank stabilization and wildlife habitat, providing contact information for assistance. It then defines "pest plants" as non-native species that disrupt indigenous ecosystems, often introduced through gardening, and highlights their negative impacts such as overwhelming native diversity and increasing fire hazards. The document also lists examples of such plants (e.g., various ivies, Giant Reed) and provides numerous resources for learning about invasive plants, weed management, and volunteer opportunities for their removal.

This partial document describes the importance of riparian corridors and the benefits of planting native vegetation along creek banks, such as stabilizing soil, filtering sediment, and cooling water for fish. It emphasizes using native plants adapted to specific local conditions and provides lists of appropriate native trees for various Marin County watersheds, including Novato, Miller, Corte Madera, Mill Valley, and San Geronimo Creeks. The document also includes a detailed "Marin County Creekside Native Plant List" categorizing ground covers, perennials, ferns, and vines by their ideal planting location on the bank (low, middle, high) and their sun exposure requirements (sunny, partial sun/shade, shade). Finally, it advises on the potential need for permits before undertaking creekside restoration projects.

nodes.title: What are Pest Plants?
Top Pest Plants in Marin
nodes.summary: The partial document covers two main topics: a list of top "pest" plants to avoid in Marin County, detailing their common names, scientific names, and typical habitats, along with a note on ubiquitous pest plants in grasslands. The second section discusses pesticides and water quality, highlighting the prevalence of insecticides in urban streams, the toxicity and environmental persistence of synthetic pyrethroids (used as replacements for older pesticides), how to identify them, and the health risks associated with permethrin-treated clothing and certain herbicides.

This partial document defines "pest plants" as non-native species introduced to California that disrupt indigenous ecosystems, often originating from gardens and subsequently escaping into natural areas. It highlights their negative impacts, such as overwhelming native diversity, replacing wildflowers, and increasing fire hazards. The document encourages avoiding the introduction of non-indigenous plants to wild areas and mentions community efforts for their removal. It also provides resources for learning more about invasive plants, weed management, and volunteer opportunities. A significant portion of the text is dedicated to listing specific "TOP “PEST” PLANTS TO AVOID IN MARIN," detailing their common names, scientific names, and typical habitats, and notes that some pest plants have become permanently ubiquitous.

nodes.title: Pesticides and Water Quality
nodes.summary: This partial document discusses the impact of pesticides on water quality, highlighting a study that found higher concentrations of insecticides in urban streams compared to agricultural ones. It focuses on pyrethroids, which are replacing older pesticides, detailing their widespread toxicity to aquatic life and their persistence in the environment. The document provides guidance on identifying pyrethroids and advises against their use, including permethrin-treated clothing, due to associated health risks like cancer. Additionally, it addresses concerns about herbicides in "weed and feed" products, linking a common weed killer to increased miscarriages and lost pregnancies.

nodes.title: Our Water–Our World
nodes.summary: This partial document promotes the use of less toxic alternatives for household and garden pest management, highlighting the "Our Water, Our World" program in Marin County. This program partners with 15 local retail stores to make safer products available, and it guides consumers on how to identify these products in stores. The document also provides extensive resources for sustainable practices, including websites for information on native plants, creating healthier habitats, further less toxic pest management alternatives, volunteering for invasive plant removal, and finding "Bay-Friendly" certified landscapers. It describes "Bay-Friendly" landscaping as a holistic approach that protects water and air quality, nurtures soil, conserves resources, and uses integrated pest management.

nodes.title: Resources
nodes.summary:
The partial document serves as a resource guide, primarily offering contact information and websites related to native plants, sustainable landscaping, and environmental stewardship. It provides resources for:
*   Information on native plants, including local and statewide societies.
*   Creating healthier habitats and implementing less toxic pest management strategies.
*   Volunteering opportunities for removing invasive plants.
*   Finding and understanding "Bay-Friendly" landscaping, a holistic and sustainable approach that protects water and air quality, nurtures soil, conserves resources, and reduces waste, specifically within the San Francisco Bay Watershed. It also provides ways to locate certified Bay-Friendly landscapers in Marin County.

structure.summary: This partial document highlights the numerous benefits of using native plants in gardening, such as their adaptation to local conditions, ability to attract beneficial wildlife, low water requirements, and resistance to pests. It clarifies the definition of a "true" native plant as one specific to a particular local area and offers practical tips for planting and establishing native plants, including initial care, ideal planting times, and long-term maintenance.
